speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want me to read it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. You can read it to anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you when.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to run the colored bars first.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. Today is October 20, 1994, and I'm going to be interviewing Handa Stark. My name is Jean Berenson, and we are in Los Angeles. And we're speaking in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like you to introduce yourself. Tell me who you are. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Handa Stark. My maiden name was Handa Josipovis. And I was born in Velky Sevlus, Czechoslovakia in 1924. I had a brother-- or I should just talk?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell me. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Tell me everything you can about your family. Tell me about your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a brother and my grandparents both from my father's side and mother's side. They lived real close, and I spent lots of time with them. I went to a Czech school with wonderful girlfriends, Jewish and non-Jewish. The town was rich in agriculture-- small, 12,000 inhabitants. Out of the 12,000, about 3,000 Jews. There was only Orthodoxy. Even the people who did not observe much, they didn't have a separate shul. There was only an Orthodox shul. Our family was Orthodox. My father prayed in a tallis and tefillin every day, except Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a physician. Very well-liked physician, because the people-- or he had a name that he never asked the patient if he has the money. First he attends to him and takes care on him. And if he can pay, he pays. If not, not. And so they liked him so much that when the time came of the ghetto, out of five physicians, he was the only one who was allowed to live in the city and didn't have to go to the ghetto. My father had nine brothers and sisters. My mother had five brothers and sisters. Most of them perished during the war. Some of the cousins survived. Our family lived a very close family life. The ones who didn't have enough-- the ones who had, supported them. My mother saw to it that her sister's children should get an education, get jobs, get married, because the sister couldn't take care of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your own schooling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the-- our president was Masaryk. He was a wonderful president. And he saw to it-- it was compulsory that every child, rich or poor, they had to go to school, at least six elementary classes he had to take. There was no such thing that a boy has to work on the farm and he doesn't go to school. They were forced to go to school. I went to six elementary schools in my town. And then I took a test, and I went to a gymnasium in another town about 60 kilometers from where I lived. I took the train every morning. And every evening I came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 11. And when the disturbances started I was only 13. Between 13 and 14 I stopped going to this gymnasium, and I continued in our town. I don't know what is the-- how would your call it? A middle school or a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a high school, because the high school was where I had to take a test to go to the high school. But this was a school that offered general education for four years, and then you graduated. From there you could go to a business college, or you could then take a test and go to the high school also. Now, after I finished the four years-- I mean, competed the four years, I went to a business school.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my town. And I only finished one year. Then they deported us-- my family and I were deported to Galicia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me, when was that? When did the disturbances start having some effect on your life in your town? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, on my life actually it was in '41. Because until then, a child feels protected when you are with your mother and father, no matter what goes on in the world. You feel that they will take care of you, and you feel protected. I did not have any bad experiences. I didn't hear of anybody in our town that would be persecuted because they were Jewish. It only started when the Germans came into Budapest. In '38, '39, we were occupied by the Hungarians. Until then, everything was fine. And then the Hungarians came in. Then it was a little different. And then the Germans came to Budapest. Then it really started to be bad. And the businesses were taken away from the people. They called it a [NON-ENGLISH]. A [NON-ENGLISH] was that he didn't have to put in any money. He didn't have to even work. And he said, all right, I'm your [NON-ENGLISH]. And he took half or whatever he wanted from the business, without doing anything for it. And then in '41, it came that you had to produce your citizenship papers. Now the citizenship papers you could produce if you showed that you owned land in a certain time long ago and you paid taxes. Then they know that you were a citizen or you were already living there that time. But my father's parents lived in a small, small village where the houses were covered with not shingles, but straw. And the office that housed the book that had all these records burned down, and they could not show that they owned land. There would have been a way how to get the papers, but my father was very busy, and he was bombarded with letters from all over France and relatives that said, please send me the papers. Please send me these papers. And he did for everybody, but not for himself. And they prolonged the period when you had to get the citizenship in order to six months. But hardly three months passed by, then came a thing that all the people who don't have the citizenship, they will be deported. And they took 500 people from the villages and from our town. And they took us to the city hall, and we were sitting there in a basement and a few hours. And then they put us on trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take anything with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little, hardly anything. Or, I think nothing, because I remembered it, I didn't have any clothes. Somebody gave me a night shirt. And out of that they made me a dress, and that was my wardrobe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents didn't take anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother and your brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my father, and my brother and I, and they-- they put us on a-- they took us to the train station and into cattle cars, and we were on our way. We went all the way to the border of Poland and Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in the cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 30 women, men, and children. And my mother, you know-- when you are 16-years-old and you are with your mother and father and you didn't have a chance to see the world before, even if you are in a cattle car-- we did have a bench to sit on. The train is rolling and you open the doors and you look out and you see beautiful forests and you see villages go by-- I can't say that-- my mother and father, I'm sure they worried. But I didn't worry one bit. And it wasn't an unpleasant experience. Then we arrived and they put us in a lumber yard. The lumber yard, it had a big, big building. [BACKGROUND NOISE] They-- they put us in cattle cars, and we arrive to this big lumber yard. And there we stayed for a whole week. Now this was not so pleasant because we didn't have where to wash. It was unpleasant to go to the bathroom. And we slept only on boards. We didn't have any bedding or nothing. And it was a Saturday, and trucks came. And they started to pile people on the trucks and take them. We didn't know where. We were left until the very, very end. And some of the richer people who were with us, they were called in, and there was a high-ranking soldier who questioned them. And they all promised him, probably something, and they were let home. My father came back and told my mother that, dear, I hope you're not angry at me, but I couldn't kiss their asses. And I didn't say nothing. And because of that, we were the very last ones. They waited, maybe we were going to break down. And they put us on the truck and we started out. And they took us all the way to the Dniester. That's a river in Poland. And while this was happening they stopped certain places, and they let us off a little. At one point, they called my father-- a woman fainted-- he should go to attend to her, which was a few trucks ahead of us. And then the soldiers, they were Hungarian soldiers, very rough and very unpleasant people. They had in their hat a feather, a rooster feather. And they were known that they were very mean and cruel. And he said that we should board the buses. And my father was coming from further up, and he sort of like pushed my father. My father fell on the bus, and my brother got very angry and said, don't you push my father! And just because of that-- and my father pacified my brother, that leave my son, you know. Nothing is happening to me. And just because of that, every time we stopped, they came and looked for my brother and threatened that they were going to do something to him. So my mother was terribly, terribly worried and upset. Anyway, we came by the night, and they put the trucks on a ferry and we went across the Dniester River and continued our trip. We came all the way to a little town in Poland that's called Jagielnica.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there-- also, they wanted to make everything very dramatic. They unloaded us by the cemetery. And next to the cemetery there was quarry. And so there was a big, big hole. And the soldiers-- the gendarmes told us, look that way. You see? There is the border. Now remember that if you come that way, we shoot you all. Don't you ever come back. And they went away. They drove away, and they left us there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the Ukrainian-- they really were not policemen. They were, I think, just people who lived there. Or some were policemen maybe, but they didn't have any uniforms. They had a gun, but they didn't have any uniforms. They came, and they told us to line up and march. And while we were going-- and we didn't know where they are taking us-- my father who spoke Ukrainian told them, that, you know, you be careful what you do with us, because the Hungarians are smart. They want that you should have the blood on your hands. They want to be clean. And you remember what happened in the first-- after the First World War, that you were left without nothing because you did bad things. Well, I don't know whether they understood or whether this meant anything to them, but they took us to a big-- I don't know if you could call it a farm, but it was a place where they had racehorses. Not now, but before. And there was a big, big barn with beautiful porcelain bowls for the horses to eat from and so on. And they housed us there. There was an attic, and there was a lower part. And the 500 people were there. Now, there was a well where we could take water and wash ourselves. The food-- we had a kettle where we boiled some potato and carrots, or I don't know what. Some little thing. And it is very, very poor, very nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there also, oh, about 10 days. And my father went into the village to get some kind of medication because we had nothing. And he was the only physician in the whole group. And they gave it to him, and they were very nice to him in the village. And also he could talk to them. There was a doctor who spoke fluently Jewish. He was not a Jew, but he spoke fluently Jewish. And he told my father that, you know, doctor-- we are so short of doctors here-- there was a typhoid epidemic-- that I would like you to stay here. Well, first my father said that, you know, I'm the only physician with the group, and I feel that I have to go with them. And he says, no, no, you talk it over with your wife, and you stay here. And then the time came that they wanted us to go further. Then this doctor sent a policeman and that they should take us out and we should stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that same village?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that same village. And we did. I don't know if you want to know, but there is a rabbi who is from [NON-ENGLISH] who is a famous rabbi. Should I tell that story? And he was there with his little son, a 6-year-old boy. And he told my father in Jewish, that, what shall I do, doctor? What will happen to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father told him that, you know, when they tell you to march, you collapse like you fainted. And then they will call me, and I will say that you have to go to the hospital. And he says, but what will happen with my luggage? What will happen with my-- he had a fur coat. And my mother said, Rabbi, if you will live, you will have another fur coat. And if you die, you don't need a fur coat. And that's how it happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took him to the hospital. And then the-- the village hid him. The Jewish people hid him in the village. And then later, from Hungary, his followers, they sent a car, and he survived. The people, unfortunately-- there was another girl that was without her parents, and my parents wanted she should stay with us. And she didn't want to. The other people, they were all taken to [NON-ENGLISH] which was a terrible place. And they lived there for a few weeks or maybe two, three months. And they were all killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were all people from your village?

speaker: interviewee
text: From our village and from around the village. And we stayed-- this doctor who took my father out, his name was Dr. [NON-ENGLISH]. And he took us to his house. He had a three-story house, and he furnished for us an apartment. He furnished for my father an office, and we lived there for a  whole year, with a little interruption. But we lived with him-- I have a picture of him. And we correspond with his family even today. He's-- he's no longer, and his wife died. But his daughter and grandchildren live, and we send them regularly packages. And my brother sends them money. My father said, a Gentile, if he does something good for a Jew, he should remember-- or he should know that a Jew never forgets. And also my father arranged that they inscribed his name in the Yad Vashem among the righteous Christians. And a delegation from Israel went to Krakow and presented his wife with a gold medal and a letter. And they were very moved and very happy about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were there for six months. And my brother wanted to go home. And my parents didn't want to let him go by himself. And we started out, and we got as far as Kolomaya. And from Kolomaya we couldn't go any further because there was a Jewish man in the Judenrat, and he said that he would not have the heart to let us go because there are Germans on the border whose sole entertainment is to catch these Jews who are strangers, who are trying to go home, and kill them one by one. Between the Hungarian and Czech border?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was already between the Polish and the Hungarian. But this was on the Polish side. And we stayed there for two weeks. And-- you want the abbreviated version? [LAUGHS] Because this is-- this didn't go so easy. We got to a town named [NON-ENGLISH]. That's a small village. And the man who  was taking us with his wagon, he didn't want to go any further because he was attracting attention. And he was afraid that the German-- the Germans will catch him, that he's transporting a Jew. And so they put us down in this down, and we didn't know how to go any further. And the man came from Kolomaya for provisions-- a Jew, with a truck, with a German truck. And we talked to this man. And I always say that he was our angel sent from God. And he took us to Kolomaya on the truck. And when we were in this building of the Judenrat where there were other people who came from the villages and they had nowheres to be, then he always came to see how we are doing. And he came, and he said that they're going to close the doors of the ghetto, and there will be no way to get out. And we had some bread and some potatoes, some beans that we took with us. And he took that and sold it, and he paid for a German car, a driver. And we sat in this car, and it was wonderful to see how all the gates opened in front of us. And we came again to the Dniester. And they came to the Dniester, and by then the Dniester was frozen. Because this was before Pesach. And we crossed on foot, the Dniester, and we came to a Jewish house. And we slept in a chair. And the next day, we still had a  little money left. We hired a wagon who took us back to Jagielnica. And then this doctor saw us. He hugged us and kissed us, and he said, now I told you not to go. And you promise you're not going to leave until the war is over. And you stay here. And everything was like before. But my brother was young, and he had a girlfriend. And he wanted to go home again. But by then, the family sent us the citizenship papers. So we felt that if we had this paper and we get a passport from the Germans that we can go, then we will be safer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what year was this would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in '42. And we hired a truck. And with 14 others-- I mean 10 others because we were four-- Jews, we started out again. And during the day we traveled. But there was the truck that had  a canvas in the back that people should not be able to see in. And we started out again. Now we came-- we came to the-- we came to the border Skole. The name was Skole. That's on the Polish side. And there the people are told us that this is a terrible place. We shouldn't stay there. The wagons are here now because it was market day from the border, and we should right away, go. And my father said, no. He's so tired that he cannot go any place. And so if we didn't go, the rest of the people didn't want to go. And Jews took us in and hid us. And later we found out that if we would have gone that day, there were Germans waiting just for people like that. And the group who went ahead of us, they killed everybody. Now we stayed here again about 10 days. It was terrible hunger. There was absolutely nothing to eat. The people were dying just from hunger. And we just couldn't take it any longer. And again, Saturday night we gathered by the rabbi who gave us a student, a rabbinical student, or a [NON-ENGLISH]. And he took us to the border. He had to follow the river and quietly, one after the other. And he told us that  as soon as we hear something, we should right away scoot down. We should right away stoop down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we had to cross the road. And we heard a wagon from far away. And we had to jump on other side of the road. There was a ditch full with water. Now there was no time to question, should I or shouldn't I? And we all jumped in until the wagon rolled by. Now what we did-- it took about two days to get to the border. Because during the day we had to hide, and we could only travel at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a little food because he knew that if we come to the border, money doesn't mean anything. And if we want somebody's help, we can pay them or the biggest thing we can give them is food. So we didn't dare to eat-- my brother and I. My mother and father, they were older, they restrained themselves. But we were very hungry. But we were told that this is it. This is the little you can eat, and that's it. And as we came down at night to continue our trip, two Ukrainian boys or Polish boys ran after us and stopped us. And they took everything away form us, everything. As children at that time I had a feeling we can travel lighter because it was heavy to schlep the things that we had on our backs. Anyway, they took everything away. And then we arrived again to a Jewish family on the border. They were very, very, very nice and very understanding. They gave us room to sleep. And all they could feed us was with oat gruel. Now I don't know if you know how oat gruel tastes. The-- the-- the flake of the oat-- the oat that we eat is already-- the outside is taken off. And what is around it is very sharp. It has a point. And to eat that, it's terrible. But that's what they had to eat. Anyway, it came night. Everybody got a walking stick, and we started to cross the border. And we crossed the border back to Hungary. And as soon as we came down from the mountain and we were going-- like here was the river, here were the houses and we were going behind them. And the-- the-- the guard duty was going on the road here. And they noticed us, and they-- they-- they're taking us back to the border. They're going to take us back to Poland. Now my father gave himself-- my father gave himself an insulin shot, which if you get an insulin shot, your nose starts running and your eyes water and you become disoriented, and it looks very bad. And when they took us to the-- to the top of the hill. Then my mother says to the Hungarian lieutenant or whatever his title was, that, look my husband is sick. He has to have a doctor. And I've got in my leg a big splinter which was black and blue and sticking out. And my daughter needs a tetanus shot. And he looks at my father and looks  at me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And says, well, what can I do? And my mother says, look, we have all the papers. I have the citizenship papers. Let me see. My mother showed him. He right away went away and called the chief of police in the next town, and they asked if we have all kind of signatures on it. And he said everything is in order. And he said, all right, the rest you send back, but these people you keep there. And my mother said to the group, do you have any rings or jewelry or necklaces? I had a little necklace with  a little pendant. She gathered all the gold stuff or watches and she took those to this man because she saw that he's a nice person. Look, these people went through so much. Take this and let them go. And sure enough, all these people, the soldiers took them to the border. They looked the other way and everybody went home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the police chief came out on a-- on a train and looked over our papers, and he said, OK. And we went been back to the village. But he still needed 10,000 pengo or whatever it was then. And we came home. And we were home about a year and a half. Our house was confiscated. We had to live at the big house. We had to live in a little house. My mother had to farm, and the horses were gone and everything. But we were alive and together with the family. And I didn't go back to school because by then things were-- Jews couldn't even go to school because this was already starting to be bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was what, like 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, end of '42. And in '43 the Germans came into to Budapest, and we were deported to Auschwitz in '44.
